findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a segment of dense, sclerotic dead bone, known as a sequestrum, which is separated from the surrounding living bone by a lucent rim of granulation tissue. On MRI, this sequestrum appears markedly hypointense on both T1- and T2-weighted sequences and does not enhance following contrast administration.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include a surrounding rim of living reactive new bone called an involucrum, which may be breached by a focal cortical defect or cloaca. Additionally, there is often extensive adjacent bone marrow edema, thick periosteal reaction, and a hyperintense enhancing rim of granulation tissue or a sinus tract extending to the skin surface.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of an expansile soft tissue mass with 'arcs and rings' calcification to suggest a primary chondroid tumor. Furthermore, there is no central enhancing nidus with a surrounding intense fusiform cortical thickening lacking a fluid collection.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a central necrotic, non-enhancing bone fragment surrounded by an involucrum and a cloaca with an associated soft tissue fluid collection.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Osteomyelitis, given the classic triad of a sequestrum, involucrum, and cloaca. The presence of marrow edema, peripheral rim enhancement representing granulation tissue, and potential sinus tract formation are characteristic of a chronic active pyogenic infection.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH), however this condition typically presents with a more 'punched-out' lytic lesion and occasionally a 'button sequestrum' in the skull, but generally lacks the thick, mature involucrum and extensive purulent cloaca seen in chronic osteomyelitis. Furthermore, LCH most often affects children and young adults and lacks the severe adjacent soft tissue inflammatory changes of profound bacterial sepsis.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Osteoid osteoma, given that its central radiolucent nidus with occasional central calcification can mimic a small sequestrum, and missing an active infection could lead to inappropriate management and progressive bone destruction. However, osteoid osteomas characteristically present with intense nocturnal pain relieved by salicylates, lack a true purulent fluid collection, and display an intensely enhancing central nidus rather than a non-enhancing necrotic sequestrum.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to define the full extent of the marrow involvement, identify any drainable soft tissue abscesses, and map any sinus tracts to guide surgical debridement.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is critical to establish the chronicity of the infection and to look for previous traumatic fractures or orthopedic surgeries that might have seeded the bone.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the affected limb if only radiographs or CT have been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the non-enhancing sequestrum, assessing the extent of intramedullary edema, and identifying subtle soft tissue fluid collections or sinus tracts.
management.mriSequences: I would specify T1-weighted sequences to define the bony anatomy and sequestrum, STIR or T2 fat-saturated sequences to demonstrate the true extent of marrow and soft-tissue edema, and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences to differentiate the non-enhancing necrotic sequestrum from the enhancing hyperemic granulation tissue and involucrum.
management.nuclearMedicine: A combined leukocyte-bone marrow scintigraphy could be considered if the MRI is contraindicated or heavily obscured by orthopedic hardware, as it helps separate superimposed active infection from sterile reactive bone changes.
management.intervention: If tissue diagnosis is needed to isolate the causative organism, I would specify an image-guided aspiration of any associated subperiosteal fluid collection or a bone biopsy targeting the active granulation tissue, ensuring the sample is sent for both histological analysis and prolonged microbiological culture.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Musculoskeletal Infection MDT. My report would clearly document the precise anatomical location of the sequestrum, the integrity of the surrounding cortex, and the path of any cloaca or sinus tract to assist the orthopedic surgeons in planning a curative resection.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a segment of dense, sclerotic dead bone, known as a sequestrum, which is separated from the surrounding living bone by a lucent rim of granulation tissue. On MRI, this sequestrum appears markedly hypointense on both T1- and T2-weighted sequences and does not enhance following contrast administration. Supporting features include a surrounding rim of living reactive new bone called an involucrum, which may be breached by a focal cortical defect or cloaca. Additionally, there is often extensive adjacent bone marrow edema, thick periosteal reaction, and a hyperintense enhancing rim of granulation tissue or a sinus tract extending to the skin surface. Importantly, there is no evidence of an expansile soft tissue mass with 'arcs and rings' calcification to suggest a primary chondroid tumor. Furthermore, there is no central enhancing nidus with a surrounding intense fusiform cortical thickening lacking a fluid collection. The feature that most influences my thinking is the presence of a central necrotic, non-enhancing bone fragment surrounded by an involucrum and a cloaca with an associated soft tissue fluid collection.

The appearances are most in keeping with Osteomyelitis, given the classic triad of a sequestrum, involucrum, and cloaca. The presence of marrow edema, peripheral rim enhancement representing granulation tissue, and potential sinus tract formation are characteristic of a chronic active pyogenic infection. The principle differential would be Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH), however this condition typically presents with a more 'punched-out' lytic lesion and occasionally a 'button sequestrum' in the skull, but generally lacks the thick, mature involucrum and extensive purulent cloaca seen in chronic osteomyelitis. Furthermore, LCH most often affects children and young adults and lacks the severe adjacent soft tissue inflammatory changes of profound bacterial sepsis. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Osteoid osteoma, given that its central radiolucent nidus with occasional central calcification can mimic a small sequestrum, and missing an active infection could lead to inappropriate management and progressive bone destruction. However, osteoid osteomas characteristically present with intense nocturnal pain relieved by salicylates, lack a true purulent fluid collection, and display an intensely enhancing central nidus rather than a non-enhancing necrotic sequestrum.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to define the full extent of the marrow involvement, identify any drainable soft tissue abscesses, and map any sinus tracts to guide surgical debridement. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is critical to establish the chronicity of the infection and to look for previous traumatic fractures or orthopedic surgeries that might have seeded the bone. I would recommend a contrast-enhanced MRI of the affected limb if only radiographs or CT have been performed, as this modality is superior here for exquisitely delineating the non-enhancing sequestrum, assessing the extent of intramedullary edema, and identifying subtle soft tissue fluid collections or sinus tracts. I would specify T1-weighted sequences to define the bony anatomy and sequestrum, STIR or T2 fat-saturated sequences to demonstrate the true extent of marrow and soft-tissue edema, and post-contrast T1-weighted sequences to differentiate the non-enhancing necrotic sequestrum from the enhancing hyperemic granulation tissue and involucrum. A combined leukocyte-bone marrow scintigraphy could be considered if the MRI is contraindicated or heavily obscured by orthopedic hardware, as it helps separate superimposed active infection from sterile reactive bone changes. If tissue diagnosis is needed to isolate the causative organism, I would specify an image-guided aspiration of any associated subperiosteal fluid collection or a bone biopsy targeting the active granulation tissue, ensuring the sample is sent for both histological analysis and prolonged microbiological culture. This should go to the Musculoskeletal Infection MDT. My report would clearly document the precise anatomical location of the sequestrum, the integrity of the surrounding cortex, and the path of any cloaca or sinus tract to assist the orthopedic surgeons in planning a curative resection.
